card.title: Why Some Chords Sound Sad
card.teaser: Change one note by a tiny amount and a happy chord collapses into a melancholy one. Here's the physics and culture behind the feeling.
card.coverImagePrompt: A grand piano in a dimly lit room, a single beam of cool blue light illuminating two keys — one major, one minor — rain streaking down a window behind, cinematic and wistful

lessons.title: A single note changes everything
lessons.body: Play C, E, and G together — you get a major chord. Bright. Open. The sound of a sunrise. Now lower the middle note by a half step to E-flat. Same hand position, almost the same notes. But suddenly it's aching, nostalgic, heavy. One note slid down by a sliver — and a whole emotion has shifted.

visual.imagePrompt: A close-up of piano keys with the C-E-G triad highlighted in warm gold, and beside it C-Eb-G in cool blue

lessons.title: Major vs. minor, in one sentence
lessons.body: A major chord is built with a 'big' third above the root note. A minor chord uses a 'small' third — just one half step lower. That tiny distance is the emotional fulcrum of Western music. Swap big for small and every mood shifts with it.

keyTerms.definition: The distance in pitch between two notes
An interval of three scale steps — 'major' if wide, 'minor' if narrow

visual.imagePrompt: A clean diagram showing a major third (4 semitones) vs. a minor third (3 semitones) stacked on a piano keyboard

lessons.title: The physics: rougher math
lessons.body: Notes are just air vibrating at specific frequencies. Major intervals line up on simple ratios like 5:4 — the air pressure waves reinforce each other cleanly. Minor intervals lean on slightly messier ratios (6:5), creating subtle interference patterns your ear registers as 'tension' or 'darkness.' It's literal acoustics: your brain hears the math as mood.

visual.imagePrompt: Two overlapping sine waves — one set smoothly reinforcing, the other producing faint rippling beats — on a black background

lessons.title: Culture adds the other half
lessons.body: Physics alone doesn't make minor 'sad.' Indian ragas and Balkan folk songs use minor-like intervals for celebration. But in Western music, centuries of lullabies, funerals, and movie scores have linked minor to loss. We now hear that association before we're even taught it — studies show Western babies as young as nine months prefer major over minor. The feeling is partly acoustic, partly cultural muscle memory.
lessons.funFact: Beethoven's 5th Symphony opens with four minor-key notes that became Hollywood's go-to signal for doom — used everywhere from horror films to 'Jaws'-style dread.

visual.imagePrompt: A collage of musicians worldwide — a violinist in Bulgaria, an Indian classical singer, a Western film composer — all connected by sheet music strands

lessons.title: Emotions are tuned, not random
lessons.body: The sadness in a minor chord isn't mystical. It's the product of overlapping sound waves, evolutionary pattern recognition, and a few centuries of cultural conditioning stacked on top. Music feels transcendent, but under the hood it's physics, neurology, and stories — and that makes it more impressive, not less.

quiz.question: What's the musical difference between a major and minor chord?
options: A third that's one half step lower in the minor chord
The minor chord uses four notes
The minor chord has a slower tempo
quiz.explanation: The third of the chord is lowered by one half step to turn major into minor.

quiz.question: Why might a minor third sound 'tenser' on a purely physical level?
options: It uses slightly more complex frequency ratios
It's played only on lower instruments
quiz.explanation: The ratio 6:5 creates more subtle interference between waves than the simpler 5:4 of a major third.

quiz.question: Is the 'sadness' of a minor chord purely universal?
options: Yes, it sounds sad in every culture
No, different musical cultures attach different emotions to minor-like intervals
quiz.explanation: The emotion of minor is partly cultural — other traditions use similar intervals for joyful or ceremonial music.

sources.title: This Is Your Brain on Music
Sweet Anticipation: Music and the Psychology of Expectation
